# Chapter 08

[1ofgpras] They left the Compound after dusk with the sun still stuck in the west like a coin melted into glass. The trucks and pods out on the highway moved with their headlamps dimmed by dust and the city’s glow sat low on the horizon. Cage drove the service van with the fake utility markings. Flynn rode shotgun with a case of tools between his feet, listening to the engine’s fan whine and the rattle of unsecured hardware behind them.

[zvoe9s2a] Diesel was in back on a fold-down bench, legs braced, checking the coil of fiber line and the little pouch of bypass keys like rosary beads. She had a headlamp around her neck but didn’t turn it on. In the dark she looked like a shape cut from the night. Birdie and Wrench were already ahead in the other vehicle, the one meant to pull them out, and their voices came and went over a cheap radio that hissed like it hated being alive.

[qg642jgv] Cage didn’t talk much on the way in. When he did it was numbers. Times. Distances. A rhythm to keep the fear in its stall. He kept tapping the steering wheel with two fingers, a soft drumming that never sped up. Flynn watched his hands and tried to see any tremor. There was none. Cage’s calm was a kind of violence all by itself.

[9tyiea6o] The Phoenix Sprawl rose out of the desert like a mirage that refused to vanish. The arcology towers were far off but the outskirts spread for miles, a mesh of gated subdivisions and warehouse parks and cul-de-sacs of identical houses turned gray by heat. The city pods floated on their lanes with their white-blue running lights, silent, obedient. Flynn kept his head down when they passed the license cameras.

[oidnv8kp] Khoury’s enclave sat behind an older wall, concrete with solar razor wire on top, the kind built in the water war years. The van rolled up to the service gate and stopped where the asphalt turned into manicured gravel. Cage leaned close to Flynn and spoke without turning his head. You’re the hands. Don’t think. Just do.

[9opa1m3x] Flynn took the tablet with the spoofed work order and held it out to the gate camera, keeping his fingers steady. The scanner blinked, thinking in its simple way. A moment later the gate buzzed and slid aside. The first win was quiet and it made his stomach lift anyway. Diesel’s boot touched the floor behind him, a single tap like punctuation.

[5heei6e5] Inside the wall everything was cooler, watered, softened. Trees planted in straight lines. Grass too green to be honest. The van crept along a private road under low lamps. It felt wrong to bring their dust and their rust into this place. Flynn saw a man jogging in a reflective vest, his breath pluming in the chilled air, and the man didn’t even look at them.

[ob8awswk] They parked in a service alcove behind a storage building that smelled of fertilizer and ozone. Cage killed the engine and the sudden absence of noise made the night feel enormous. He pulled on gloves, flexed his fingers, and smiled once in the dark. It wasn’t for Flynn. It was for the job itself.

[lohpa2ci] They moved on foot, three shadows crossing trimmed gravel and stepping over irrigation hoses. Diesel led them to a low maintenance door at the back of the garage complex. Cameras covered the obvious approaches, but she’d mapped the blind angles from the drone footage and from the little walks she’d taken days ago wearing a uniform and a cap, carrying nothing but a mop bucket and a face that didn’t get remembered.

[bspoalic] Flynn knelt by the junction panel beside the door. The metal cover was cool under his fingertips. He slid his pick into the seam and pried it open, exposing the neat rows of labeled circuits. The system was modern, redundant, expensive. It made him angry in a childish way. He clipped on his bridge and waited for the tiny green light to go solid. The alarm loop sighed and went to sleep.

[uyt0re7h] Cage’s hand brushed Flynn’s shoulder, not gentle but precise, moving him aside. Cage slipped the latch with a tool that looked like a sliver of bone. The door opened without complaint. Air spilled out smelling of polished leather and cold metal, a museum smell. Flynn followed them in and his skin prickled, as if the building itself were watching.

[cs82otlj] The garage corridor was lit dimly, the light tuned to keep rubber from aging and paint from fading. Display plaques sat on waist-high stands. Flynn saw dates and maker names and some of the words meant nothing to him, but the money in the place was obvious. He’d grown up around cheap screens that were thrown away and cars that drove themselves. Here the past was kept like a pet.

[opykwuxd] Diesel walked faster, eyes moving over the space, taking inventory. Cage drifted as if he belonged, hands behind his back, head tilted like a man touring his own collection. Flynn kept catching reflections of himself in the glass—thin, dirty, pupils wide—and behind him the cars shone, perfect, their curves like animals asleep.

[0eog1t9i] They reached the central bay and it opened into a wide room with rows of vehicles under soft covers, each cover weighted at the corners. Some were lifted on stands like offerings. The floor was white epoxy without a single oil stain. Cage stopped and breathed in through his nose. Flynn heard it, the little huff, like satisfaction.

[t9ex3qaf] The Shelby sat uncovered near the back, as if even the cloth was too much between it and worship. White paint, blue stripes, the nose blunt and arrogant. It wasn’t the newest thing in the room but it had a gravity the others didn’t. Flynn had seen pictures, grainy, in old forums that the city filters tried to bury. The real thing looked alive.

[47og2pmy] Diesel was already at the driver’s side, rolling a low cart of tools she’d pushed out from a hidden service niche. She popped the hood with a practiced motion and the latch made a sound too loud in Flynn’s head. Underneath, the engine bay was immaculate, but immaculate wasn’t the same as ready. A machine could sleep itself to death.

[8r3mkylh] Flynn went to the security pedestal mounted near the bay doors, the one that controlled the internal sensors. His job was to blind the room without tripping the system’s heart monitor back at the guard station. He brought up the maintenance interface through his bridge, fingers hovering in the air, and for a second he felt the same illicit joy he’d felt when he first cracked school firewalls, when rules were just puzzles.

[yhfdeajz] Cage crouched by the front wheel and ran his thumb over the tire sidewall like a man checking fruit. He whispered something to the car that Flynn couldn’t hear. It made Flynn’s scalp crawl. Diesel glanced at Cage, her mouth set, and then she went back to work with the clipped efficiency of someone refusing to watch a ritual.

[2b1zdigg] The ignition system was old and new at once, updated to satisfy the collector’s insurance requirements. Diesel pulled a small module from her pocket and slotted it into the wiring harness. She listened with her fingers, feeling for the pulse. Then she turned the key, not all the way, letting the circuits wake. The dash lights came on like a restrained dawn.

[50cwsgvt] The first crank was a cough. The second was a deeper cough, wet and unwilling. Flynn held his breath and tasted oil that wasn’t even in the air. Diesel adjusted something with a screwdriver, small movements, not guessing. On the third crank the engine caught and rolled into a low idle that filled the room with a sound Flynn had only heard in contraband clips. It was a throat clearing after decades of silence.

[v28vk58q] The noise was wrong in that clean place, obscene. It made the cars around it seem to flinch under their covers. Flynn felt his chest loosen with relief and fear at the same time. Cage stood up and smiled again, and in the glow from the dash his teeth looked too white.

[8g7xn1p7] A door somewhere down the corridor opened. Not the one they’d come through. A soft beep followed, the kind of sound people didn’t make on purpose. Diesel’s head snapped toward it, listening. Cage’s body went still. Flynn’s fingers froze above his interface, the maintenance screen shimmering like a lie.

[z3go9cqg] The guard stepped into the bay doorway with a flashlight, swinging the beam slow. He wore a uniform and a sidearm and a look of mild irritation, like he’d been pulled from a warm chair. The beam caught the Shelby’s running lights and the man’s expression changed from annoyance to confusion. His mouth opened to ask a question and no sound came out.

[qkn6g53o] Cage moved first. He didn’t run. He walked fast, straight at the guard, hands empty and low, like he was going to explain. Flynn thought, stupidly, maybe Cage will talk him down. Maybe this is what the rehearsals were for. Cage got within arm’s reach and then his fist came up and hit the guard in the throat.

[jnu3fbd0] The guard folded, gagging, flashlight clattering on the floor and spinning its beam across the walls. Cage caught him by the collar and slammed him into the doorframe. The sound was wood on bone. Flynn saw the guard’s eyes go wide with surprise more than pain. Diesel shouted Cage’s name once, sharp, like a tool dropped on concrete.

[umgs3c3f] Cage didn’t stop. He hit the guard again, and again, quick, economical blows that weren’t about knocking someone out. He drove the man down to his knees and kept punching, the gloves dulling the sound but not the force. Flynn watched blood mist the air in a fine spray and mark the white epoxy floor like a confession.

[9grwqfqe] Flynn’s body wouldn’t move. His mind kept offering little excuses like coins. The man has an implant. He’ll be back. This is just the rules here. But the guard made a noise like a broken animal and it ripped through Flynn’s rationalizing. He saw Cage’s face in the strobing flashlight beam, calm, concentrated, almost serene.

[vqul8ct4] Diesel grabbed Cage’s shoulder and tried to pull him off. Cage shrugged her away without looking, like she was wind. Diesel swung her fist and struck Cage on the side of the head. It wasn’t hard enough to do damage but it was hard enough to be real. Cage turned then, slow, and his eyes met hers with something like amusement.

[ntr1lwed] Diesel said Stop. Just that word, not a plea, an order. Cage stared at her for a breath that felt long. Then he looked back at the guard and delivered one more punch, deliberate, as if finishing a thought. The guard went limp, face sliding sideways into the clean floor. His chest still rose, shallow, and blood pooled under his cheek.

[eh37iyw7] Cage stood up, breathing through his nose, and wiped his glove on the guard’s uniform. He glanced at Flynn like he’d forgotten he was there. The look on Cage’s face wasn’t anger. It was satisfaction, pure and uncomplicated. Flynn felt cold despite the conditioned air.

[s7h85qh9] The alarm didn’t scream. Flynn had done his job. The building stayed quiet except for the Shelby’s idle and the faint gurgle from the guard’s throat. Diesel’s jaw was clenched so tight Flynn could see the muscles jump. Cage nodded toward the bay doors, and the nod meant Move, as if nothing had happened.

[u0q27yd7] Flynn forced his hands to work. He killed the internal sensors, spoofed the bay door status, and ran the routine that would make the system think it had all been a scheduled maintenance cycle. His interface showed little green checkmarks like approval. He wanted to smash the pedestal. He wanted to vomit. He did neither.

[asdzkygo] Diesel got in the Shelby and put it in gear. The car rolled forward with a smoothness that made Flynn think of a knife sliding out of a sheath. Cage took the wheel of the tool cart and pushed it aside, clearing their path. Flynn looked back once at the guard. The man’s flashlight beam had settled on the ceiling, illuminating nothing.

[oe5no2ae] They drove the Shelby out through the service bay, the door lifting just enough and then closing behind them. Outside, the night air was warmer and smelled of wet lawn. The engine sound was suddenly free, spilling into the rich quiet neighborhood, and for a second Flynn thought every house would wake and point at them. But the walls held, and the sound was swallowed.

[sxqx6q28] Birdie’s voice came over the radio, urgent and thin. You got it? Cage answered with a calm Yes. Wrench said Two minutes. Headlights at the far intersection. Flynn heard distant pod traffic, a whispering tide. Diesel drove without lights, following the line of dark trees, trusting her memory more than her eyes.

[cttrb7uc] They met the extraction vehicle in a shadowed service lane behind a row of hedges. Wrench was already out, tailgate down, ramps set. Birdie’s face was bright with excitement, and then Flynn saw the smear of blood on Cage’s glove and Birdie’s grin wavered, confused, but he didn’t ask. Cage didn’t offer anything.

[hx1a1rt8] The transfer was quick. Diesel eased the Shelby up onto the trailer with a gentle touch, as if coaxing an animal into a cage. Flynn helped strap the wheels, his hands slippery with sweat. Cage stood to the side watching the neighborhood like a king expecting tribute. Flynn noticed, with a small shock, that Cage’s breathing had barely changed.

[jdn3hxm0] They were moving again within minutes, vehicles merging back onto the edge highways where the cameras were older and easier to fool. The trailer swayed behind Wrench’s truck. Flynn sat in the van and stared at his own reflection in the windshield, superimposed over the road. His eyes looked wrong. He kept seeing the guard’s mouth opening, the moment before the first hit.

[hyud0llb] Cage talked now, a low stream of instructions and praise. Good work. Clean. Easy. He said Flynn had hands like a surgeon. Flynn nodded because that was what was expected. In the mirror Cage’s eyes were bright, alive in a way Flynn couldn’t share. The difference between them felt like a distance you couldn’t drive.

[0u8zlv7y] When they reached the Compound the sun was almost up, bleaching the desert at the edges. The crew moved like people coming off a binge, laughing too loud, touching the Shelby’s fenders with reverence. Birdie circled it and kept saying Jesus. Diesel didn’t laugh. She went straight to the workshop sink and scrubbed her hands until her knuckles turned red.

[gdkxmagc] Cage called them all in close, as if for a family photo, and spoke about the race like it was already won. The guard was fine, he said, shrugging. The system would revive him. He’d wake up with a headache and a new story to tell his friends. Cage spread his hands as if offering proof. No harm done. This world fixed itself.

[62ndvkv8] Flynn went to his container and lay on the cot with his boots still on. He listened to the Compound settle, to the generator’s steady chug, to someone outside laughing, to a tool clinking. Every time he closed his eyes he saw Cage’s face in the flashlight beam, the calm joy of it. He tried to tell himself the guard would wake up. He tried to believe that made it clean. Sleep wouldn’t come.
